species: Orca echolocation full sequence -- click trains + signature whistles + breach impact
scientific_name: Orcinus orca -- Southern Resident Killer Whale (SRKW) population, pods J, K, L
sound_type: Three-component full acoustic repertoire. (1) Echolocation click trains: 40-120 kHz (ultrasonic, hydrophone required), source level 160-220 dB re 1 µPa, scan rate 1-2/sec, buzz rate 300+/sec at prey capture approach, detection range 100-300m. (2) Signature whistles + pod calls: 1-18 kHz (audible range), individual-specific signatures + pod-specific discrete calls (dialects), social coordination and cohesion. (3) Breach impact: adult male 6-8m 6000-8000 kg, underwater impact 120-160 dB re 1 µPa (wideband), airborne splash 80-100 dB at 100m, audible several miles. Sequential event: not simultaneous -- clicks during approach, calls during social activity, breach during behavioral sequence. THE MOST COMPLETE ORCA ACOUSTIC DOCUMENTATION IN THE ENGINE.
ecosystem: Narrow channel, Pacific Northwest inland marine waters. Primary: Haro Strait (San Juan Island WA / Vancouver Island BC), 3-8 km wide, 50-100+ m deep. Also: Boundary Pass, Rosario Strait, Puget Sound. Channel geometry creates waveguide effect enhancing horizontal sound propagation. Chinook salmon primary prey species (Oncorhynchus tshawytscha).
season: Resident (SRKW J-pod year-round in Salish Sea; K and L pods range to outer coast seasonally). Peak salmon-chase activity: summer (Chinook salmon runs).
family: Delphinidae (oceanic dolphins). Orca is the largest member of the dolphin family. Order Cetacea, Suborder Odontoceti (toothed whales).
milestone: MOST COMPLETE ORCA ACOUSTIC DOCUMENTATION IN ENGINE. Full three-component sequence: click trains (biological sonar, 40-120 kHz), signature whistles + pod calls (social communication, 1-18 kHz), breach impact (physical percussion, 120+ dB wideband). Southern Resident Killer Whale population: 73 individuals (2023), ESA endangered, 3 pods (J K L), every individual identified in photo-ID catalog since 1976 (Center for Whale Research). Tahlequah (J35): 17-day calf-carrying 2018, worldwide attention to SRKW crisis. The buzz: 300+ clicks/sec prey approach, Chinook salmon cannot detect above ~1 kHz -- orca sonar completely invisible to primary prey. Haro Strait: acoustic waveguide enhancing call propagation.

coordinates.description: Haro Strait, San Juan Island / Vancouver Island boundary. 48.52°N 123.15°W. Primary SRKW feeding corridor and acoustic research site. Center for Whale Research based on San Juan Island.

acoustic_data.click_trains: Frequency: 40-120 kHz (ultrasonic). Source level: 160-220 dB re 1 µPa at 1m (among loudest biological sounds in ocean). Production: phonic lips (nasal passage) + melon (fatty forehead lens, 20-30 degree beam). Reception: panbone / jaw fat body. Scan: 1-2 clicks/sec. Buzz: 300+/sec at prey approach. Detection range: 100-300m depending on target and conditions.
acoustic_data.calls_whistles: Frequency: 1-18 kHz (audible range). Types: (1) signature whistles (individual-specific), (2) discrete calls (pod-specific dialects, all pod members share). SRKW dialects first documented Ford & Ellis 1970s-80s. Function: social coordination, cohesion, individual recognition. Audible to surface observers at very close range. Channel waveguide extends propagation range.
acoustic_data.breach: Mechanics: full body exit at 5-8 m/s vertical velocity, rotation airborne, re-entry maximum surface contact. Adult male: 6-8m body length, 6000-8000 kg. Impact: 120-160 dB re 1 µPa underwater (wideband). Airborne: 80-100 dB at 100m. Audible several miles. Function: contested (communication, parasite removal, prey herding, social bonding, play). Often serial (multiple pod members).
acoustic_data.e7_calibration: E=7 through full-sequence integration. No single component alone: clicks are ultrasonic and inaudible without equipment; calls are modest amplitude at surface distance; breach is brief and infrequent. Three components together, experienced as a complete close-encounter sequence in Haro Strait: E=7. The most complete orca acoustic documentation in the engine.

nodes.title: Full Three-Component Orca Acoustic Repertoire at E=7
nodes.description: Click trains (biological sonar, ultrasonic) + signature whistles/pod calls (social communication, audible) + breach impact (physical percussion, wideband). The most complete orca acoustic documentation in the engine. Sequential event experienced as complete narrative during close-encounter observation.

nodes.title: The Buzz -- 300+ Clicks/Sec at Prey Capture Approach
nodes.description: As orca approaches Chinook salmon to within 10-30m, click rate accelerates to 300+ clicks/sec. The 'buzz' -- audible through hydrophone as chainsaw-like sound. Chinook salmon cannot detect sounds above ~1 kHz; orca sonar at 40-120 kHz is completely invisible to primary prey. The predator's acoustic advantage with no evolutionary countermeasure in prey.

nodes.title: SRKW Population Crisis -- 73 Individuals, Declining
nodes.description: Southern Resident Killer Whale: peak 98 individuals (1995), ~73 (2023). ESA endangered 2005. Primary limiting factor: Chinook salmon scarcity. Every individual identified in Center for Whale Research photo-ID catalog since 1976. Tahlequah (J35) 17-day calf-carrying 2018: global attention to SRKW crisis. The most studied wild orca population; the most at-risk.

nodes.title: Haro Strait as Acoustic Waveguide
nodes.description: Narrow channel geometry (3-8 km wide, 50-100+ m deep) creates waveguide effect for horizontal sound propagation. SRKW calls travel farther and with less distortion in Haro Strait than in open ocean. The channel is an acoustic amplifier for the orca's social communication system and for the research program that documents it.

cross_references.relationship: E=7 + E=7 = E=14. Three-component orca acoustic sequence (clicks, whistles, breach) paired with Helms Alee's three-component musical architecture (guitar/vocals, bass/vocals, drums/vocals). Both systems use multiple simultaneous or sequential channels to communicate complex information. Layered multi-component communication in both music and marine mammal behavior.
E=8 cetacean encounter (two species, one channel) vs. E=7 single-species full repertoire. Different complexity: cross-species acoustic interaction vs. complete intra-species repertoire documentation.

Orca greeting ceremony (E=8, collective social intensity) vs. full echolocation sequence (E=7, individual complete repertoire). The ceremony: maximum social collective energy. The sequence: maximum individual acoustic range.
